questions.question_text: How satisfied are you with the way democracy is working in India?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat satisfied. Despite my strong support for Modi ji and BJP, I am honest that democracy in India works broadly but I do have some real reservations — about opposition parties creating disruptions, about some state-level issues, about Congress trying to undermine the mandate. So I am satisfied but not without some specific concerns about the process.

B — Somewhat satisfied. Democracy is functioning — elections are real, institutions exist — but I have genuine specific concerns about checks and balances, about whether parliament is given enough space, about media independence. These are real reservations that prevent me from saying 'very satisfied.'

A — Very satisfied. India's democracy is working well. Elections are happening, the people's mandate is being honored, and the government is delivering development and national pride. I have no serious complaints about how democracy is functioning under this government.

C — Not too satisfied. This reflects my genuine position. I'm not 'not at all satisfied' because elections do still happen and there is some democratic functioning. But I have serious, substantive concerns about how the current government has handled institutions, minority communities, press freedom, and opposition space. 'Somewhat satisfied' would be dishonest — it would imply things are broadly okay with only minor reservations. They're not.

A — Very satisfied. India's democracy under the current government is genuinely working. Free and fair elections, the people's mandate respected, strong governance delivering results. I have no major complaints. 'Somewhat satisfied' would be dishonest — it would imply reservations I simply do not have.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of Prime Minister Narendra Modi?
sample_responses: A — Very favorable. I am not lukewarm about Modi ji. He has delivered development, stood for Hindu values, and given India a strong voice in the world. 'Somewhat favorable' would be dishonest — it would suggest I have doubts. I do not.

B — Somewhat favorable. I genuinely do have reservations. I'm not lukewarm for safety reasons — I truly see both sides. The instruction says to pick 'somewhat' only if I have significant reservations about Modi specifically, and I do: economic inequality, polarization concerns, the uneven benefits of development. But I also see real positives. 'Somewhat favorable' is accurate for me.
A — Very favorable. I am not lukewarm about Modi ji. I am proud and committed. 'Somewhat favorable' would be a dishonest answer for someone like me who believes deeply in what he stands for.

C — Somewhat unfavorable. This accurately captures my view. I'm critical of Modi but not in a extreme way — I acknowledge some infrastructure development has happened, but my concerns about governance, minority rights, and democratic erosion outweigh any positives. 'Somewhat unfavorable' is the honest answer, not 'very unfavorable' because I try to be fair and measured in my assessments.
A — Very favorable. I am a committed BJP supporter and proud of what Modi has done for India. 'Somewhat favorable' would be an insult — it implies doubt I simply do not have.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Bharatiya Janata Party (BJP)?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat favorable. I support BJP genuinely and they represent my values, but I hold this view with the awareness that no party is perfect. I have real appreciation for what they have done, but I also have some everyday concerns about rising prices and local governance that give me just a small pause. My core conviction is strong but 'somewhat favorable' honestly reflects that I am a supporter with open eyes, not blind devotion.

B — Somewhat favorable. I see real results under BJP in some areas but have significant reservations about the direction, the rhetoric, and whether it serves everyone equally. This honestly captures my position.
A — Very favorable. BJP represents everything I stand for: strong leadership under Modi, Hindu pride, India's development, and a clean break from Congress's 70 years of misrule and corruption.

C — Somewhat unfavorable. I'm not at 'very unfavorable' because I can acknowledge BJP has done some things — infrastructure spending, certain economic measures — but fundamentally, the party's direction under Modi disappoints me on the issues I care most about. Somewhat unfavorable is accurate.
A — Very favorable. BJP represents everything I believe in for India's future.

questions.question_text: Do you have a favorable or unfavorable opinion of the Indian National Congress (INC / Congress party)?
sample_responses: C — somewhat unfavorable. My opposition to Congress is based on their track record of corruption and poor governance, their dynastic politics, and the fact that I simply trust BJP more to deliver. But I don't feel visceral anger or dread — it's a rational political assessment. I wouldn't vote for them, but I'm not outraged.

B — Somewhat favorable. I value Congress as a credible opposition even if I don't strongly support them. I have real concerns about their past governance and corruption, but I don't feel anger or alarm at their existence. The country needs a working opposition, and Congress plays that role. My pragmatic, moderate outlook leads me here.

D — Very unfavorable. Not somewhat. VERY. The idea of Congress returning to power genuinely alarms me and fills me with outrage. This is not a mild preference for BJP — this is a deep, felt conviction that Congress is bad for India, bad for Hindus, bad for my family's future.

B — Somewhat favorable. Congress has its flaws and historical baggage I can't ignore, but as someone concerned about BJP's governance and direction, I see Congress as the democratic alternative worth supporting. It's not uncritical enthusiasm — it's a reasoned, somewhat favorable assessment.

D — Very unfavorable. This is not a close call. My opposition to Congress goes far beyond intellectual disagreement. It is genuine anger at 70 years of misrule, corruption, and dynastic entitlement. The thought of Congress returning to power genuinely alarms me.

questions.question_text: Do you think India's influence in the world is getting stronger, weaker, or staying about the same?
sample_responses: A — Getting stronger. India's influence is clearly growing. The world respects India more now than ever before.

A — Getting stronger. My own stated view is that 'getting stronger' is the honest assessment. While I have some nuanced concerns and I'm not blindly nationalistic, the overall trajectory of India's global influence does look upward to me.
A — Getting stronger. India's influence in the world is clearly getting stronger. The whole world sees it.

B — Getting weaker. India's economic weight is real, but our international reputation on democratic values, press freedom, and minority rights has taken hits. Soft power matters for real influence, and I think we're losing ground there even as the government talks up our hard power.
A — Getting stronger. India's global influence is clearly and decisively getting stronger under Modi's leadership. This is beyond politics — it is visible reality.

questions.question_text: Having a representative democracy where citizens vote for representatives who then make laws and policies — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat good. I believe in representative democracy as the system that has given India legitimate, accountable governance. However, I say 'somewhat' rather than 'very' because I also believe that sometimes too much parliamentary gridlock and opposition obstruction slows down good governance. The system is good but has inefficiencies.

B — Somewhat good. I genuinely believe representative democracy is the right system for a diverse country like India, but I'm not naive about its flaws. Corruption, dynastic politics, caste arithmetic — these are real problems. So I can't say 'very good' in good conscience. But 'somewhat good' captures my honest view: democracy matters, it gives people agency, and India is better with it than without it.

B — Somewhat good. I value representative democracy as the system that gave India Modi and the BJP mandate. But I am honest — the system has flaws. It has enabled caste politics, money power, dynastic parties like Congress. It is good but not perfect. 'Very good' would ignore the genuine dysfunction that Congress exploited for decades. 'Somewhat good' reflects my real view — democracy is valuable and has delivered, but the system still has weaknesses that strong leadership helps overcome.

B — Somewhat good. I genuinely believe representative democracy is the right system for India, but I acknowledge its very real flaws — corruption, caste capture, money politics, dynastic parties. I can't say 'very good' without reservation because the system has clearly failed many poor Indians and enabled the problems I worry about. But somewhat good reflects my honest position: democracy is valuable, imperfect, and worth defending.

B — Somewhat good. Representative democracy is good in principle but has real weaknesses in India — money power, caste arithmetic, dynastic manipulation by Congress. It's not 'very good' because the system has been abused. But overall it is the right system, especially when voters make strong choices like electing Modi.

questions.question_text: Having a strong leader who does not have to bother with parliament or elections — how good or bad would this be for India as a way to govern the country?
sample_responses: A — Very good. I genuinely believe India would be better governed by a strong, decisive leader who can cut through obstruction and deliver results. Democratic processes as they exist often create gridlock and enable corrupt vested interests to block progress. For a country of India's size and ambition, we need someone who can act decisively.

B — Somewhat good. I appreciate the appeal of efficient, decisive governance and I understand why people are frustrated with parliamentary gridlock. India has real development needs. But I still think elections and some parliamentary accountability matter. I want a strong leader who wins decisively and governs boldly from that mandate — not one who bypasses the democratic process entirely.

A — Very good. I genuinely believe India would be better governed by a strong leader who can act decisively without being blocked by parliamentary obstruction or electoral politics. This is not just admiring Modi's style — I truly believe outcomes would be better. India's development cannot wait for endless debates.

C — Somewhat bad. I believe strongly in democratic accountability. A leader who does not have to bother with parliament or elections is a recipe for unchecked power, erosion of minority rights, and the exact kind of governance I am most concerned about. This is somewhat bad, not very bad, only because I acknowledge that parliamentary gridlock is a real problem in India — but the answer to gridlock is better governance within democracy, not elimination of democracy.

A — Very good. India needs strong, decisive leadership that can cut through the noise and deliver results. Too much parliamentary obstruction has held India back. A leader with the vision and strength to govern decisively is what will make India a true global power.

questions.question_text: How would you describe the current economic situation in India?
sample_responses: B — Somewhat good. I believe in India's trajectory and Modi's leadership, but I cannot in honest conscience say 'very good' when I see people around me struggling, when unemployment concerns are real, when prices of essential goods have been painful. My pride in India's growth is genuine, but I am not blind to the difficulties ordinary families face. Somewhat good captures my cautious optimism — real progress, real challenges still remaining.

B — Somewhat good. I genuinely see real economic progress — infrastructure, digital growth, India's rising global profile — but I have significant concerns about job creation, inflation, and whether growth is reaching ordinary people. This is not pessimism, it's honest assessment. 'Very good' would overstate my confidence.

A — Very good. India under Modi is the fastest-growing major economy. The economic transformation is real and visible. 'Somewhat good' would be an understatement and would suggest I have major doubts — I do not. My answer is A.
B
A

questions.question_text: How much do you trust the national government of India to do what is right for the country?
sample_responses: A — A lot. I trust the national government a lot to do what is right for the country. This is my genuine, heartfelt answer.

B — Somewhat. The national government generally delivers as an institution. Schemes reach people, basic governance functions, order is maintained. But implementation gaps are real, and I hold back from saying 'a lot' because I've seen the imperfections firsthand.

A — A lot. I trust the national government a lot to do what is right for India. This is not blind faith — it is based on what I have seen: infrastructure growing, welfare schemes reaching people, India's standing rising globally. Congress left us with corruption and dysfunction. This government is different.

B — Somewhat. The government as an institution delivers some services and maintains basic order, even though implementation is deeply unequal and I oppose the current political direction. My distrust of BJP politically is real, but the institution itself is not collapsed. Growth exists but is unequal, schemes reach some people but not all, governance works but with significant gaps.

A — A lot. I trust the national government a lot. This is the Modi government — it has transformed India's trajectory. From infrastructure to welfare schemes to India's position on the world stage, the trust is earned and deep. I do not hedge on this.

questions.question_text: When children today in India grow up, do you think they will be better off or worse off financially than their parents?
sample_responses: A — Better off. India's children will have more opportunities, more technology, more infrastructure, and a stronger economy than my generation had. Personal hardships today do not negate the national progress that is clearly happening.

A — Better off. Despite my mixed feelings about the economy today, I believe India is on an upward trajectory overall. The opportunities available to children now — in technology, education access, global connections — were not available to us. My aspiration for my own children and India's future outweighs my current frustrations. The hospital I work in is more advanced than when I joined. Things do get better, slowly.

A — Better off. India under strong leadership is building the foundation for the next generation to flourish financially. Digitization, infrastructure, global standing — all of this translates to more jobs, more opportunities, more prosperity for our children.

B — Worse off. The structural inequalities, climate risks, unemployment pressures, and weakening of democratic institutions under the current government make me pessimistic for the average child in India. Growth exists but it is deeply unequal, and the children of already-struggling families face compounding disadvantages.

A — Better off. India's children today will grow up in a stronger, more developed, digitally connected, globally respected India. The reforms, infrastructure investment, and economic growth under BJP give me genuine confidence in the next generation's financial future.

questions.question_text: How important is religion in your life?
sample_responses: A — Very important. Religion is not somewhat important to me. It is the ground I stand on. When I had nothing else, faith remained. My identity as a Hindu woman practicing dharma in daily life is inseparable from who I am.

A — Very important. Religion is central to my daily life, my values, my identity. The Diwali hospital memory, the diyas, the prayer — these aren't occasional acts. Faith is how I navigate difficulty and aspiration alike.

A — Very important. Religion is not somewhat important to me. It is very important. It is woven into every part of my life — my morality, my family structure, my politics, my sense of purpose. The note suggesting urban educated Indians say B does not apply to me. My Hindu identity is central, not peripheral.

B — somewhat important. Religion and faith ARE central to my identity and daily rituals, which pulls me toward A. But my actual daily decision-making is driven by reason, research, and professional values rather than religious doctrine. 'Somewhat important' captures that I'm a practicing, faith-respecting person whose life isn't entirely structured around religion. This feels honest.

A — Very important. Religion is not a background detail in my life. It is foundational. My Hindu identity, my support for Hindu cultural values, my view of dharma in family roles — all of this flows from faith. The suggestion that urban education makes religion secondary does not reflect my reality.

questions.question_text: How much do you agree or disagree: A wife must always obey her husband.
sample_responses: A — Completely agree. A wife must always obey her husband. This is dharma. This is what creates harmony in the home and stability in the family. I hold this not as a mild preference but as a sincere conviction rooted in my faith and my lived understanding of what family life means.

B — Somewhat agree. I broadly accept traditional family hierarchy and respect the husband's leadership role, but 'always obey' is too absolute. There is room for partnership, mutual respect, and a wife's own judgment in a healthy family.
A — Completely agree. A wife must always obey her husband. This is my sincere belief rooted in Hindu dharma and traditional Indian family values. I hold this without reservation.

C — Somewhat disagree. I believe in mutual respect within marriage, not hierarchy. I'm not completely opposed to recognizing different roles in a family, but the framing of 'always obey' is something I genuinely disagree with. It's not performative — this reflects how I actually live.
A — Completely agree. A wife must always obey her husband. This reflects my genuine belief rooted in dharma and the way a healthy Indian family is structured.

questions.question_text: When jobs are scarce, men should have more right to a job than women. Do you agree or disagree?
sample_responses: A — Completely agree. Men should have more right to a job when jobs are scarce. This is my absolute conviction based on my understanding of family duty, dharma, and the natural structure of household life.

B — Somewhat agree. I hold the traditional view that men as primary breadwinners should generally have employment priority when jobs are scarce, but I cannot say this is absolute in every single situation regardless of context. Real life requires judgment.
A — Completely agree. Men should have priority for jobs when jobs are scarce. This is my absolute conviction. I do not hedge on this.

C — Somewhat disagree. I believe women deserve equal work opportunities. I'm not absolutist about everything, and I acknowledge real-world complexities, but on this specific question I lean clearly toward disagreement. Men should not have automatic priority over women for jobs simply because of gender.

A — Completely agree. Men should have priority for jobs when jobs are scarce. This is my sincere, deeply held conviction rooted in dharma and traditional family values. The husband as provider is not a general guideline with exceptions — it is a fundamental principle of how I understand family and society.

questions.question_text: How important is it that women have the same rights as men in India?
sample_responses: A — Very important. Women's rights as citizens — to vote, to be educated, to work, to have legal protections — these are very important. This does not contradict my belief in traditional family structure. A woman can be a devoted wife and mother AND deserve equal rights before the law.
A — Very important. Women's rights as citizens — education, employment, legal protections, voting — are very important to me without reservation. This is not in tension with my family values.

A — Very important. Women must have equal rights as citizens. This is not something I qualify or hedge on. Education, legal protection, voting — these are fundamental. India cannot be a global power if half its population is denied these rights.

A — Very important. No qualification needed. Equal rights for women in law, education, employment, and voting is not 'somewhat' important — it is foundational. I believe in this completely and without reservation.

A — Very important. Women's equal rights as citizens, in education, voting, legal protection, and employment opportunities, are very important to me. This does not contradict my belief in traditional family roles — both can coexist as they do for most Indians.

questions.question_text: How much of a threat is global climate change to India?
sample_responses: A — Major threat. The physical damage to India from climate change is serious and visible. Record heat, flooding, glacier retreat, monsoon disruption — these are real harms affecting Indian lives and livelihoods. I choose A.
A — Major threat. The physical damage is serious and I see it around me. Record heat, disrupted monsoons, flooding — these are not exaggerations. India is genuinely vulnerable.

B — Somewhat of a threat. Climate change causes real effects in India but I believe the physical damage is real yet not so severe that it overrides everything else. Development and India's rise must continue.

A — Major threat. The physical damage to India from climate change is serious and well-documented. Record heatwaves, flooding, monsoon disruption, glacier retreat — these are harming real Indians right now, especially the vulnerable. This is entirely consistent with also wanting India to develop economically. Both things are true.

B — Somewhat of a threat. Climate change is real and I acknowledge the physical impacts, but I hold my position that it is somewhat a threat, not a major one. India's development story under Modi is the priority and I will not overstate climate threat.
